revision.comment: Sort [[Template:Expand language]] by topic
revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

[[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia44]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

[[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia44]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

[[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia44]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dardar]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:kk]] Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Moe (slang)]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Moe (slang)]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Inari temples]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:1 revision imported]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[category:Ryōhō-ji]][[category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]]{{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}}

Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[temple]] affiliated with [[Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010年11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]]
