revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] affiliated with [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji]] in western [[Tōkyō]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|qq=}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[sn:Ryōhō-ji]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[sn:Ryōhō-ji]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

revision.comment: Bot: append |1= |comment= |WD= in {{ill}}
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|qid=Q206611}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|qid=Q208863}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11371049]] Wikidata name: Ryōhō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] affiliated with [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji]] in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|qq=|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[sn:Ryōhō-ji]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|qq=|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}}

[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|qq=|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! 了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] affiliated with [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji]] in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|qq=|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371049]] Wikidata name: Ryōhō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] affiliated with [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji]] in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371049]] Wikidata name: Ryōhō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] affiliated with [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji]] in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371049]] Wikidata name: Ryōhō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji

[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Inari temples]] }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tōkyō]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] affiliated with [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren-shū]] located in the city of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji]] in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of [[:en:Nichiren Buddhism|Nichiren Buddhism]]. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] * [[:en:Cemetery|Cemetery]] * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|[[:en:Moe (slang)|Moe]]-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in [[:en:Cosplay|Cosplay]], taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble [[:en:IOSYS|IOSYS]].[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371049]] Wikidata name: Ryōhō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tōkyō}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū}} | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple}} affiliated with {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū}} located in the city of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji}} in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|WD=Q871371|lt=Nichiren Buddhism}}. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} * {{ill|Cemetery|en|Cemetery|WD=Q39614|lt=Cemetery}} * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|{{ill|Moe (slang)|en|Moe (slang)|WD=Q497942|lt=Moe}}-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble {{ill|IOSYS|en|IOSYS|WD=Q512251|lt=IOSYS}}.[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371049]] Wikidata name: Ryōhō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tōkyō}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū}} | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple}} affiliated with {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū}} located in the city of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji}} in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|WD=Q871371|lt=Nichiren Buddhism}}. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} * {{ill|Cemetery|en|Cemetery|WD=Q39614|lt=Cemetery}} * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|{{ill|Moe (slang)|en|Moe (slang)|WD=Q497942|lt=Moe}}-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble {{ill|IOSYS|en|IOSYS|WD=Q512251|lt=IOSYS}}.[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Category]] *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|WD=Q871371|lt=Nichiren Buddhism}}. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} * {{ill|Cemetery|en|Cemetery|WD=Q39614|lt=Cemetery}} * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|{{ill|Moe (slang)|en|Moe (slang)|WD=Q497942|lt=Moe}}-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble {{ill|IOSYS|en|IOSYS|WD=Q512251|lt=IOSYS}}.[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Category]] *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|{{ill|Moe (slang)|en|Moe (slang)|WD=Q497942|lt=Moe|1=Moe (slang)}}-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay|1=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble {{ill|IOSYS|en|IOSYS|WD=Q512251|lt=IOSYS|1=IOSYS}}.[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863|1=Hachiōji}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tōkyō|1=Hachiōji}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū|1=Nichiren-shū}} | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} affiliated with {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū|1=Nichiren-shū}} located in the city of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji|1=Hachiōji}} in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|WD=Q871371|lt=Nichiren Buddhism|1=Nichiren Buddhism}}. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} * {{ill|Cemetery|en|Cemetery|WD=Q39614|lt=Cemetery|1=Cemetery}} * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|{{ill|Moe (slang)|en|Moe (slang)|WD=Q497942|lt=Moe|1=Moe (slang)}}-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay|1=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble {{ill|IOSYS|en|IOSYS|WD=Q512251|lt=IOSYS|1=IOSYS}}.[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[co:commons:Category:Ryōhōji]][[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] [[en:Ryōhō-ji]][[ja:了法寺]][[ko:료호지]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Ryohoji Temple.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}}|prefecture={{ill|Hachioji|WD=Q208863|1=Hachiōji}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.660594|N|139.316194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in western Tokyo, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ryōhō-ji
了法寺 | native_name = | image = Ryohoji -01.jpg | image_size = 250 | alt = | caption = Ryōhō-ji main hall | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ryōhō-ji | location = 2-1 Hiyoshi-chō, {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tōkyō|1=Hachiōji}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|38.1|N|139|18|58.3|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū|1=Nichiren-shū}} | deity = Lotus Mandala | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{Official|1=http://ryohoji.jp/}} | founded_by = Keiun Nicchō | year_completed = 1489 }} {{nihongo||了法寺|'''Ryōhō-ji'''}} is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} affiliated with {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|WD=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū|1=Nichiren-shū}} located in the city of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji|1=Hachiōji}} in western [[:en:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its ''mountain name'' is {{nihongo||松栄山|'''Shōei-zan'''}}. The temple is among those of the {{transl|ja|Hachiōji [[Seven Lucky Gods|Shichifukujin]]}} Pilgrimage ({{lang|ja|八王子七福神めぐり}}), and enshrines the goddess [[Benzaiten]]. It is popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え寺|'''Moe-ji'''}}.{{cite news |date=2009-11-18 |title=注目ワード "萌え寺" 了法寺 |language=Japanese |trans-title= |newspaper=[[:en:読売新聞|読売新聞]] 夕刊 |page=9}} It was formerly associated with {{transl|ja|[[Honkoku-ji]]}} ({{lang|ja|本圀寺}}) of the {{transl|ja|Rokujōmon-ryū}} ({{lang|ja|六条門流}}) branch of {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|WD=Q871371|lt=Nichiren Buddhism|1=Nichiren Buddhism}}. ==History== *1489 - The temple was established by Keiun Nicchō ({{lang|ja|啓運日澄}}) for his retirement. *1490 - Establishment formerly recognized within {{transl|ja|Hachiōji}}. *1590 - Relocated to {{transl|ja|Hiyoshi-chō}} ==Temple grounds== * {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} * {{ill|Cemetery|en|Cemetery|WD=Q39614|lt=Cemetery|1=Cemetery}} * [[Inari Hall]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷堂}}, {{transl|ja|Inari-dō}}) *Ema shelf ({{lang|ja|絵馬掛}}, {{transl|ja|Ema-kake}}) *Picture guide ({{lang|ja|案内看板}}, {{transl|ja|Annai-kaban}}), popularly known as {{nihongo||萌え看板|Moe-kaban}} ==Faith tradition== As is customary of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] tradition, [[Shinto]] [[kami]] are also enshrined at Ryōhō-ji such as [[Ugajin]], an agricultural deity closely associated with the Buddhist deity [[Benzaiten]]. {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} and [[Ukanomitama]] are both enshrined within the Inari Hall. The temple has also enshrined [[Daikokuten]] and [[Ryūjin]] in an effort to expand its popularity. ==Moe-ji== In May 2009, the temple installed an illustrated signboard featuring Buddhist and Shinto deities as anime characters, prompting the popular name {{nihongo||萌え寺|{{ill|Moe (slang)|en|Moe (slang)|WD=Q497942|lt=Moe|1=Moe (slang)}}-ji}}.{{cite web |last=Thomas |first=Jolyon Baraka |date=2015 |title=Welcome to Ryohoji! |website=Tricycle: The Buddhist Review |url=https://tricycle.org/magazine/welcome-ryohoji/ |accessdate=2019-12-27}} In order to provide a cheerful atmosphere for visitors, the enshrined {{nihongo|Shingo Benzaiten|新護弁財天}}, an eccentric, stylized form of the goddess, is presented through various forms of music and entertainment. The temple abbot was introduced to voice actress and illustrator {{ill|Toromi|ja|とろ美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toromi|1=Toromi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11273544}}, who went on to play the role of Benzaiten in {{ill|Cosplay|en|Cosplay|WD=Q142554|lt=Cosplay|1=Cosplay}}, taking on the persona "Toro Benten."{{cite web |last=Ashcraft |first=Brian |date=2011 |title=Praying To The Anime Goddess |website=Kotaku |url=https://kotaku.com/praying-to-the-anime-goddess-5742461 |accessdate=2019-12-27}} August 2010 saw the release of a theme song for Ryōhō-ji, {{nihongo||寺ズッキュン!愛の了法寺!|Tera zukkyun! Ai no Ryōhō-ji!}}, under the Ryōhō-j Records label created by music ensemble {{ill|IOSYS|en|IOSYS|WD=Q512251|lt=IOSYS|1=IOSYS}}.[http://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/f-et-tp0-20101107-699570.html 八王子の「萌え寺」で奉納祭] 日刊スポーツ 2010th year 11月14日閲覧 [[File:Toro-benten.JPG|thumb|[[Toromi]] cosplaying as [[Benzaiten]] and singing]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] *[http://ryohoji.jp/ 了法寺] Official website *[https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfWKSvy-4RPNEZtZylFolrA かみたま 元了法寺] YouTube channel {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Buddhist-temple-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} [[en:Ryōhō-ji]] {{Commons category|Ryōhōji}} [[ja:了法寺]] [[ko:료호지]] {{wikidata link|Q11371049}} [[Category:15th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiōji, Tokyo]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
